data.title: Gentex forecasts 2027 revenue of $2.8B-$2.9B while raising 2026 gross margin guidance to 34.5%-35.5%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Gentex Corporation (GNTX) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “For the second quarter, Gentex reported net sales of $651.3 million, down 1% from $657.9 million in the second quarter of last year.” (President, CEO & Director Steven Downing)
  • “Automotive revenue declined approximately 3% year-over-year, reflecting lower revenue in Europe, Japan, Korea and China, which was largely offset by strength in North America.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “In China, revenue remained under pressure as ongoing tariff-related market disruptions contributed to a 20% year-over-year decline.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “Premium audio revenue increased 16% year-over-year to $51.7 million.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “In total, the company received approximately $38 million of IEEPA tariff reimbursements during the quarter, of which roughly $18 million reduced cost of goods sold and favorably impacted gross margin.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “While revenue came in below our forecast, disciplined execution across the business enabled Gentex to deliver record second quarter earnings per share of $0.54.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “During the second quarter of 2026, the company repurchased 2.7 million shares of its common stock at an average price of $24.48 per share for a total of $66 million.” (VP of Finance, CFO, Treasurer & Chief Accounting Officer Kevin Nash)
  • “Shipments through the first half of 2026 have positioned us to deliver on our estimated growth rate of 200,000 to 400,000 units over prior year that we projected at the beginning of the year.” (COO & CTO Neil Boehm)
  • “Gentex is in process of setting up a plant in Morocco… and are making progress in support of a targeted start of production in 2028.” (COO & CTO Boehm)

Outlook

  • “Consolidated revenue is still expected to be between $2.65 billion and $2.75 billion.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “We are raising gross margin guidance and now estimate it to be between 34.5% and 35.5% for the year.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “We are lowering our operating expenses budget to be between $405 million to $415 million for the year.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “We are also lowering our tax rate estimate to be between 16% and 17%.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “We are also lowering our capital expenditures estimate to be between $115 million and $125 million for the year.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “The updated guidance reflects the anticipated impact of all known tariffs effective as of yesterday.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “Based on the mid-July 2026 Mobility Global light vehicle production forecast… the company continues to expect calendar year 2027 revenue to be in the range of $2.8 billion and $2.9 billion.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)

Financial Results

  • “Gross margin for the second quarter was 37% compared to 34.2% in the second quarter of last year, representing an increase of 280 basis points.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “Consolidated operating expenses for the second quarter were $99.7 million compared to $106.8 million in the second quarter of last year.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “Income from operations for the second quarter was $141.3 million, up 19% from $118.5 million in the second quarter of last year.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “Net income attributable to Gentex was $114.7 million, up 19% from $96 million in the second quarter of last year.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “Diluted earnings per share were $0.54 compared to $0.43 in the second quarter of last year.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • “For the second quarter, preliminary cash flow from operations was $180.9 million compared to $166.1 million in the second quarter of '25.” (VP of Finance, CFO Nash)
  • “Second quarter free cash flow reached $161.7 million… and year-to-date free cash flow totaled $281.8 million.” (VP of Finance, CFO Nash)

Q&A

  • Joseph Spak, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: “So the $20 million that went to the balance sheet, do you have that coming through gross margin in the back half of the year…?” Kevin Nash: “That goes against inventory… it’s really an effective reduction of inventory.”
  • Joseph Spak, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: “How should we think about a good level for that interior European mirrors business going forward?” President, CEO & Director Downing: “You got 2 distinct factors… the China impact… And then… some lost programs on the Volkswagen side… [and]… European customers… struggling with their volumes.”
  • Joseph Spak, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: “Can you talk about any revenue opportunity associated with that?” President, CEO & Director Downing: “The first award, we believe, will be probably between $100 million and $200 million.”
  • Davis Baker, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division: “Can you walk us through the customer conversations supporting your Morocco investment?” President, CEO & Director Downing: “Yes, we have several customer commitments already… The first step… is going to be a transition of final assembly products from… the U.S. to the Morocco facility.”
  • Davis Baker, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division: “Update on the internal large area device efforts…?” COO & CTO Boehm: “We’ve made some really good progress over the last 6 months… testing is still looking really good.”
  • James Picariello, BNP Paribas, Research Division: “Is there a point of stabilization [in China]…?” President, CEO & Director Downing: “No, it’s still in decline… I’d say it’s probably right around $100 million would be where we’ll end the year.”
  • Josh Nichols, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: “Is 35% type gross margin going to be a floor…?” President, CEO & Director Downing: “I wouldn’t say it’s a floor… probably that 34% to 35% is kind of the sweet spot.”
  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: “Are you expecting additional pressure… before 2028?” President, CEO & Director Downing: “If we hadn’t made that plan… there would have been risk… But with our plan, we feel very comfortable.”
  • David Whiston, Morningstar Inc., Research Division: “Is there more of those [IEEPA refunds] coming…?” Kevin Nash: “This is the lion’s share of it… anything else is going to be incremental.”
  • Rajat Gupta, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: “What’s giving the confidence in the second half reacceleration?” President, CEO & Director Downing: “There are some pretty strong product launches… additional FDM… and… ramp… on… DMS and ICMS launches.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • “Slightly negative” analyst tone was centered on Europe base-mirror volume declines, China trajectory, and how much of the tariff reimbursement is repeatable, including: “Is there a point of stabilization here…? Or is it still in decline?” (James Picariello, BNP Paribas, Research Division)
  • “Slightly positive” management tone in prepared remarks emphasized profitability and execution despite lower-than-forecast revenue: “disciplined execution… enabled Gentex to deliver record second quarter earnings per share.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • Relative to the prior quarter, management’s language showed more specificity on localization actions and award timing (Morocco SOP and U.S. electronics manufacturing award) and continued caution on tariffs and inputs: “we tend to be a little conservative because this merry-go-round has gone round and round.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 introduced a defined localization timeline and scope in Europe: “setting up a plant in Morocco… targeted start of production in 2028,” compared with Q1’s broader localization framing: “demand for localized production.” (COO & CTO Boehm)
  • Q2 guidance shifted versus Q1 by raising gross margin to 34.5%-35.5% and lowering operating expenses, tax rate, and capex, while keeping 2026 revenue and 2027 revenue range unchanged. (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • Q2 made the IEEPA tariff impact concrete with cash received: “received approximately $38 million,” versus Q1’s uncertainty: “has not recognized any potential refund.” (President, CEO & Director Downing)
  • Q2 analyst questions concentrated on Europe base-mirror declines and China outlook, while Q1 questions leaned more toward RFQ status and timing for the electronics contract manufacturing initiative. (Analyst Q&A)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Ongoing tariff-related market disruptions” in China and localization requirements in Europe were cited as demand and share headwinds, with mitigation tied to content growth (FDM, DMS, ICMS) and regional manufacturing moves. (President, CEO & Director Downing; COO & CTO Boehm)
  • “Shortages on the electronics side” and “raw material costs” were flagged as second-half headwinds, alongside tariff uncertainty, with management pointing to operational efficiency and launch cadence as offsets. (President, CEO & Director Downing)

Final Takeaway

Gentex’s Q2 call emphasized margin and cash performance supported by IEEPA reimbursements and product mix, while management framed China as “still in decline” and highlighted steps to protect Europe business through a Morocco plant targeted for 2028 production. Guidance kept 2026 revenue unchanged but raised gross margin and lowered operating expense, tax, and capex ranges, while reiterating $2.8 billion to $2.9 billion of expected 2027 revenue and outlining a U.S. advanced electronics manufacturing award opportunity of “between $100 million and $200 million.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Albertsons not moving fast enough to lower prices and lure shoppers from Walmart, Aldi — BMO
data.description:
Albertsons Companies’ (ACI) shares are limping to the end of the week nearly 27% lower from a week ago after the grocery chain missed profit guidance and slashed its full-year outlook to reflect a more cautious consumer environment and soft industry trends.

"While [fiscal first quarter] results did not meet our expectations, they underscored the need to move faster," said CEO Susan Morris. "We are accelerating change to strengthen our competitiveness and improve execution across the business," she added.

Unfortunately, BMO’s Kelly Bania doesn’t think Albertsons is moving fast enough on cost reductions to fund better pricing, “leaving us to worry that future profitability remains at risk.”  

As a result, Bania downgraded Albertsons (ACI) to Market Perform from Outperform and cut her target price by nearly half to $12 to more accurately reflect the repricing of the stock. She also lowered her fiscal second quarter EPS estimate to $0.29 from $0.43 and FY27 to $1.78 from $2.22. 

“Our May 2025 upgrade was clearly not the right call,” Bania wrote in her note to clients, basing the earlier ratings change on early signs of EBITDA stabilization that would help improve valuation.

“However, it’s clear ACI is not moving fast enough to provide a more compelling value offering against a fast-changing food retail backdrop,” specifically with lower-income consumers who continue to migrate to Walmart (WMT), Amazon (AMZN), and Aldi.

Given signs of increased pricing competition and the weak industry backdrop, there is a risk of continued EBITDA decay for Albertsons (ACI), which should lead the grocer to consider strategic alternatives. Given its unionized workforce, Bania sees limited M&A options—especially after the failed Kroger (KR) merger. That leaves asset sales as a remaining option.

data.title: Eli Lilly and Sanofi-backed Scribe surges in public debut
data.description:
Scribe Therapeutics (SCTX), a gene therapy developer whose collaborators include Sanofi (SNY) and Eli Lilly (LLY), rose nearly 50% in its first trading session as a public company on Friday after an upsized IPO that raised $128.7M in gross proceeds.  

Shares of the Bay Area biotech opened at $25 each at about 11:45 am ET, indicating more than a 66% rise from its IPO price of $15, which was at the top end of the marketed range of $13–$15 per share. The stock reached $22.19 at about 1:25 pm ET, implying a 47.9% gain.

Late Thursday, the company priced the offering of roughly 8.6M shares of its common stock, up from ~7.2M it previously proposed to offer ahead of its trading debut on Nasdaq. Scribe (SCTX) disclosed in a filing that its partners Eli Lilly (LLY) and Sanofi (SNY) agreed to buy additional stock at the IPO price.

Sanofi (SNY) agreed to purchase approximately 7.5M shares in a concurrent private placement, and Lilly (LLY) indicated its intention to raise its stake to about 11% by buying additional stock after the private placement and IPO.

Underwriters have a 30-day option to buy up to an additional ~1.3M shares at the IPO price.

data.title: Tariffs are boosting inflation, slowing economic activity – The Conference Board’s Erin McLaughlin
data.description:
Tariffs are fueling inflation while simultaneously slowing economic activity, according to Erin McLaughlin, economist at The Conference Board.

The trade policy uncertainty is creating significant headwinds for businesses planning long-term investments and keeping markets cautious, McLaughlin said in a Bloomberg podcast interview.

Her analysis of North American trade revealed that year-over-year volumes are down for almost every commodity except computer parts and aircraft (JETS), with steel (SLX) declining 20% while computer parts rose 25%.

Overall U.S. tariff rates are now projected to reach approximately 13% following recent increases, she noted.

The economist characterized these tariffs primarily as a negotiation strategy rather than a meaningful tool for addressing the federal deficit or encouraging onshoring.

"Threatening 50% on Canada for a lot of these commodities and materials and stringing our neighbors along is definitely not making the case for nearshoring and onshoring," McLaughlin said.

She pointed out that the U.S. is the only country among the three USMCA partners that is opting to go year-to-year rather than reaffirming the agreement for another decade.

This policy approach creates significant unpredictability for companies considering manufacturing investments.

McLaughlin noted that a one-year truce with China took effect in November, and discussions starting in September could potentially reestablish some certainty in trade relations.

Beyond tariffs, McLaughlin pointed to structural issues weighing on the American economy, particularly in housing.

"When people buy houses, they buy a lot of other things," she said, noting that the average age of first-time homebuyers has risen to 40 from 33, delaying purchases and "life decisions that trigger spending."

More on tariffs

  • Trump's tariff plan eases retailers' worst-case fears
  • Countries respond to new U.S. tariff blitz based on Section 301
  • Trump to hit 60 trading partners with new tariffs on Friday over forced labor
  • China is said to seek new Boeing order terms, raising doubts on Trump-Xi deal
  • Trump targets generic drug imports with up to 200% phased tariffs

data.title: MarineMax jumps on report Blackstone, Donerail among final bidders
data.description:
MarineMax (HZO) quickly rose 6.5% after a report that Blackstone (BX) and Donerail are among the final bidders for the recreational yacht retailer.

The two investment firms, as well as financial sponsor Centerbridge, are in the final round of bidding for MarineMax (HZO), according to a Reuters report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

MarineMax, Blackstone, and Donerail declined to comment to Reuters. A representative for Centerbridge didn't immediately respond to a comment request.

The latest update comes after Reuters reported in May that the recreational yacht retailer agreed to go forward with a sales process, and one of the bidders has increased its offer. Donerail, which owns a 5% stake in MarineMax (HZO), proposed to buy the company for $35 a share in cash in February and subsequently raised its offer.

On Thursday, MarineMax (HZO)  reported results that missed top-line and bottom-line estimates and reaffirmed the FY26 outlook.

data.title: Tobacco's smoke-free pivot: Who holds the regulatory edge?
data.description:
Investors Should Know: The smoke-free nicotine transition is reshaping revenue mix and regulatory standing across major tobacco companies. Industry leaders like Philip Morris International (PM), British American Tobacco (BTI), and Altria (MO) are each staking out distinct positions in heated tobacco, oral nicotine pouches, and vaping.

Background

Tobacco manufacturers have spent the past decade pivoting away from combustible cigarettes toward smoke-free alternatives. The three main product categories driving this shift are heated tobacco devices, oral nicotine pouches, and vaping products. Each carries a different regulatory profile and growth trajectory.

FDA authorization has emerged as a key competitive dividing line. Regulators have granted marketing authorization for select smoke-free products, including IQOS devices, ZYN nicotine pouches, and Swedish Match's General snus. These authorizations allow companies to make reduced-risk marketing claims that competitors without authorization cannot legally make.

Stocks to Watch

Philip Morris International (PM) leads the smoke-free transition by revenue scale. British American Tobacco's (BTI) Velo oral nicotine pouch brand and Altria's (MO) on! pouch brand are the other major publicly traded players with meaningful smoke-free exposure. Japan Tobacco (JAPAF) and Imperial Brands (IMBBY) round out the group of globally listed tobacco names with smoke-free operations.

Reynolds Consumer Products (REYN) operates in the broader consumer products space with tobacco-adjacent household product exposure. Cronos Group (CRON), which has ties to Altria, sits at the intersection of tobacco and cannabis markets.

Key Takeaways

  • Philip Morris International's smoke-free segment now accounts for 42% of total net revenues, with smoke-free products available in 109 markets and international H1 organic revenue growth of 13.7%.
  • FDA regulatory authorizations, including Modified Risk Tobacco Product designations for ZYN and marketing authorization for IQOS, are creating differentiated marketing rights that not all competitors have secured.
  • Altria and BTI are both growing oral nicotine pouch volumes, but the broader pouch category is expanding faster than either company's individual brand share, keeping competitive pressure elevated.
  • Across the group, Altria posts the highest net and EBITDA margins, while Japan Tobacco leads on revenue growth at 10.92% year-over-year.

News Roundup

Recent earnings results and regulatory developments illustrate how differently each company is navigating the smoke-free transition. The items below span quarterly results, FDA milestones, and product launches that are directly shaping the competitive landscape.

  • Philip Morris International (PM) — PM reported Q2 2026 net revenue of $11.19 billion, beating consensus by $550 million, with smoke-free revenue growing 11.7% year-over-year and shipments up 7.5%, driven primarily by IQOS. Non-GAAP EPS of $2.20 beat estimates by $0.15.
  • Altria (MO) — Altria's on!PLUS became the first product authorized under the FDA's pilot program streamlining reviews for oral nicotine pouches, shipping nationwide in March and reaching approximately 100,000 stores. The on! brand grew shipments 17.6%, though the broader nicotine pouch market, now representing more than 58% of total oral tobacco, grew faster, resulting in category share loss for on!.
  • British American Tobacco (BTI) — According to figures cited by Seeking Alpha contributor The Asian Investor, management projects Velo revenue will grow nearly fourfold, from £1.2 billion in FY2025 to £4.3 billion by decade-end, targeting 15% average annual pouch-category growth. Velo adoption is accelerating across Scandinavia, Mexico, Kenya, and Pakistan, though BTI's combustible segment continues to see mid-single-digit volume declines, particularly in the U.S.

Smoke-Free Stocks in Focus

Expanding the scope a bit, the screener below identifies companies aligned with the cannabis theme, a category that overlaps with tobacco-adjacent nicotine and plant-based consumer products. Companies are ranked by relevance to the theme, placing the most closely aligned names first.

The cannabis thematic screener returns ten companies, spanning large multi-state operators to smaller Canadian licensed producers. Year-to-date price returns across the group range widely, with some names posting double-digit gains and others seeing significant declines. Market capitalizations range from under $200 million to over $2 billion.

The Household Products screener returns six companies with Strong Buy or Buy Quant Ratings. Reynolds Consumer Products is the only name in this universe with direct tobacco-adjacent operations. The remaining names span household and personal care product categories, with market caps and ratings distributed across the buy-rated tier.

Companies in the cannabis markets screener include Glass House Brands Inc. (GLAS), Green Thumb Industries Inc. (GTBIF), Trulieve Cannabis Corp. (TRLV), Canopy Growth Corporation (CGC), Curaleaf Holdings, Inc. (CURLF), High Tide Inc. (HITI), Aurora Cannabis Inc. (ACB), Cresco Labs Inc. (CRLBF), Cronos Group Inc. data.title: SLB soars 10% on strong activity outside Middle East war disruptions
data.description:
SLB (SLB) up 10.4% in Friday's trading, making it one of the two top gainers on the S&P 500, after topping Wall Street expectations for Q2 adjusted earnings and saying it expects Q3 revenue to increase 3%-4% Q/Q under its base-case scenario, with a gradual recovery in the Middle East.

Q2 profit fell to $786M, or $0.52/share, from $1.01B, or $0.74/share, in the year-earlier quarter, while adjusted earnings of $0.55/share exceeded the $0.51 FactSet analyst consensus estimate.

Q2 revenue rose 5% Y/Y to a better-than-expected $8.97B, as North America revenue jumped nearly 36% to $2.24B, offsetting international revenue, which declined 2.6% to $6.67B.

For H2 2026, SLB (SLB) projected materially higher free cash flow and said it expects more contract awards in the coming months that would strengthen its market position in the Middle East, which was the company's biggest market in 2025, accounting for 34% of its revenue.

Q2 revenue from the Middle East and Asia fell 14% ​to $2.57B, hit by lower activity and operational disruptions associated with the Iran conflict, but SLB (SLB) CEO Olivier Le Peuch said on the company's earnings conference call that activity is picking up pace in the U.A.E., Qatar, and to some extent in Saudi Arabia.

The impact of the war came in at the lower end of the company's guidance of $0.06-$0.08/share, helped by temporary cost actions, but "the timing ‌of ⁠a full recovery remains uncertain and will depend on a durable resolution of the conflict. As activity improves, we expect the return to full production capacity to take time," Le Peuch said, according to Reuters.

"Customers are eager to restore production," the CEO ​said, and if the conflict ​does not escalate further, he ⁠expects a "gradual recovery" unfolding in Q3.

SLB's (SLB) data center solutions unit is on track to exceed $1B in annualized revenue run rate by the end of the year, driven by rising demand and customer expansion, Le Peuch also said.

data.title: Warsh should hike interest rates ‘when he can, not when he must’ – Renaissance Macro’s Neil Dutta
data.description:
Neil Dutta, an economist at Renaissance Macro Research, is advising Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh to raise interest rates at the upcoming meeting rather than waiting, arguing that acting now during a favorable tactical window is preferable to being forced into a hike later under less favorable circumstances.

"If you're Kevin Warsh, I think it's important to go hike when you can and maintain some modicum of control over the committee than to hike when you must," Dutta said in an interview with Bloomberg TV.

Dutta pointed to several structural pressures supporting his case for an immediate rate hike, including surging AI-related capital expenditures from hyperscalers, ongoing geopolitical conflicts keeping oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) elevated, and continued tariff uncertainty.

He noted that gas prices are likely to rise heading into August regardless of near-term oil price movements, making the inflationary outlook challenging in the months ahead.

The strategist emphasized the tactical advantage for Warsh in moving now rather than later. While the Fed chair could likely corral the committee to hold rates steady in July, by September he may not have that luxury.

"Can you imagine a situation where he—if he wanted to, he could probably get the committee to get on board for a hold in July. By September, he may not be so fortunate," Dutta explained, adding that being forced to hike under duress "would make him look like a much weaker Fed chair."

Despite his analytical expectation of a hike, Dutta acknowledged his personal views are more dovish, citing concerns about an uneven economy.

He noted that while capital markets discuss broadening out, the real economy tells a different story, with non-AI-related structures investment remaining weak and residential investment still sluggish.

"Monetary policy is a blunt tool," he cautioned, warning that hiking rates to cool AI demand could exacerbate stresses in credit-sensitive areas like housing.

Dutta also analyzed the internal dynamics of the Federal Open Market Committee, noting that hawkish members like Beth Hammack and Lori Logan have high conviction and are “price makers” who could pull along less committed members like Philip Jefferson and Lisa Cook.

He added a political dimension to his argument, observing that President Trump has been deferential to the Fed and currently has "much bigger fish to fry," potentially giving Warsh a pass on a July hike.

"By September, or October, when you're much closer to the midterms, maybe he won't feel as forgiving," Dutta said. "So, again, that's another reason for him to get it out of the way now."

More on the U.S. economy

  • ‘Blue collar’ semis drop 21% from June highs, here is what it says about the economy
  • New home sales growth in June tops consensus; supply contracts
  • U.S. PMI Composite rises less than estimated in July flash reading
  • Countries respond to new U.S. tariff blitz based on Section 301
  • Trump to hit 60 trading partners with new tariffs on Friday over forced labor

data.title: Intel signs deal with Lens Tech for advanced semiconductor packaging
data.description:
Intel (INTC) announced on Friday that it has signed a deal with Chinese tech firm Lens Technology for advanced semiconductor packaging.

As part of the strategic collaboration, the two firms will explore glass substrate-based packaging, aimed at high performance, interconnect density, and power efficiency.

Intel will provide semiconductor architecture and advanced packaging, while Lens Tech is contributing the glass materials processing, laser manufacturing, and production.

Terms of the deal were not disclosed.

“Advanced packaging is becoming a critical driver of semiconductor innovation as AI systems continue to push the boundaries of performance, scale, and efficiency,” said Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan in a statement. “Intel brings deep expertise in semiconductor architecture and advanced packaging technologies, while Lens Technology contributes world-class capabilities in precision glass processing, laser manufacturing, and large-scale production. Together, we have an opportunity to explore new approaches to advanced packaging that can help unlock the next generation of system-level innovation for the AI era.”

“Lens Technology has built its leadership on advanced materials engineering and precision manufacturing, serving some of the world's most demanding technology companies,” June Chau, founder and chairwoman of Lens Technology, added. “By combining Lens Technology's expertise in glass materials, high-precision laser processing, and advanced manufacturing with Intel's leadership in semiconductor design and packaging, we believe we can help accelerate innovation in advanced packaging technologies. This collaboration will support the rapid development of next-generation computing solutions and AI infrastructure for customers around the world.”

The two firms said there could be other future areas of collaboration, including on AI PC components, robotics, data center server thermal solutions, and edge computing.

Intel shares were modestly lower in midday trading on Friday after the semiconductor company reported second-quarter results.

data.title: Comstock targets 25% capacity start in August as it expects $20M cash from mining asset sale
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Comstock Inc. (LODE) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CFO Judd Merrill framed Q2 as the start of broad execution, saying, "This quarter, we have begun executing on virtually every major objective we laid out at the beginning of the year," and highlighted balance sheet strength with "approximately $31.4 million of cash and no debt" and working capital of "$39.9 million."

  • CFO Merrill said Comstock "expect another $20 million in cash in August once we close on the securities purchase agreement and sell 100% of our legacy mining assets to Mackay Precious Metals," adding that the transaction "will also remove all mining reclamation liabilities and related bonding requirements and associated costs" while "retaining an upside in both the NSR royalties across the district and retaining meaningful equity in Mackay."

  • CEO Corrado De Gasperis detailed the ramp plan for the first industry-scale recycling facility, stating, "what we intend to start running immediately here in August, is at about 25% capacity," and added that "September, October, November, December, as far as our plans are concerned, will be at least 25% capacity."

  • CEO De Gasperis disclosed acceleration of downstream and metals recovery initiatives, saying, "we're moving very fast on deploying a 1 ton per day metal recovery system this year," and described it as "a pilot" intended to validate silver extraction economics and scalability.

Outlook

  • Management did not provide revenue or EPS guidance in the prepared remarks or Q&A; CEO De Gasperis reiterated on ramp expectations, saying, "We're not going to guide past running this 25% level."

  • The ramp language was tied to profitability thresholds discussed on the call: CFO Merrill said, "Once we reach 20 to 20-plus percent, we're generating cash from an operation standpoint," and added, "once we start hitting that 40%, 50% of operations just from plant #1, we're covering all the cost, and we start generating cash."

  • Compared with Q1, the company shifted from stating expectations to be "operating" in June/July to acknowledging slippage while keeping an August start; CEO De Gasperis said, "we are a few months later than we thought in terms of bringing the big machine online, even though it's imminent now."

Financial Results

  • CFO Merrill reported quarter-end liquidity and working capital, saying the company ended Q2 with "approximately $31.4 million of cash and no debt" and "total working capital of $39.9 million." He also said the company’s investments carrying value rose despite a charge: "even after recording this quarter, a noncash nonstrategic impairment... the carrying value of our investments increased to approximately $67 million."

  • CFO Merrill described first-half cash sources and early commercial traction for metals, citing "$2.6 million solar panel recycling revenue" and adding that "the sales in June and continuing even in this month of July, were about double the rate that we experienced in May."

  • CFO Merrill outlined major first-half uses of cash, including "approximately $21 million" invested into Sierra Springs, "approximately $5 million" to complete the first industry-scale metals recycling facility, "another $1.3 million" for product upgrades, "approximately $1.4 million" to advance metals recovery technologies, and "approximately $3 million metals operating costs" to ramp operations.

Q&A

  • Zach Spencer asked about dilution and share count; CFO Judd Merrill responded, "in the number of outstanding shares, no," and added, "a lot of the CapEx spend is behind us" and the company is "just focused now on executing."

  • Zach Spencer asked, "how long until this company starts making money"; CFO Merrill said, "we have been making money from the pilot operations" and tied cash flow inflection to utilization, stating, "Once we reach 20 to 20-plus percent, we're generating cash" and at "40%, 50%... we're covering all the cost."

  • Zach Spencer asked about competitors and profitability durability; CEO Corrado De Gasperis said alternatives include landfill and improper handling, and added, "we do not see anyone who has a science-based solution that can effectively generate clean materials and scale, we don't see it."

  • Zach Spencer pressed on the delayed start vs. prior expectations; CEO De Gasperis said, "We will be done testing the oven by the end of July," and added, "In August, we will start feeding a panel in the front and material coming out the back."

  • Zach Spencer asked, "What are the potential revenue and profit margins from the Illuminate agreement?" CEO De Gasperis responded, "So we wouldn't ever disclose that," and described OEM scrap as "very enhancing to our metal yields."

  • Zach Spencer asked about Bioleum updates and impairments; CEO De Gasperis said, "The impairments... were noncash, nonstrategic," and later characterized fundraising timing as "a pause, resume September, October, high confidence," while also noting, "Disappointing... But no less excited about the potential."

  • Zach Spencer asked about outage robustness; CEO De Gasperis said the company added gas-generated electricity and redundancy, stating, "we bought 2 generators, not 1," and concluded, "I think we're well protected."

  • Zach Spencer asked about ramp expectations into year-end; CEO De Gasperis said, "we feel very confident running at the 25% level," and connected milestones to profitability discussion: "getting to 50%... We're profitable as a corporation."

  • Zach Spencer asked for the timeline for Ohio and California transfer utilization; CEO De Gasperis said, "we do not right now have a time line" for starting Ohio processing and disclosed total inventory: "we've got somewhere between 8,000 to 9,000 tons of panels on the ground."

  • Zach Spencer asked about Sierra Springs power; CEO De Gasperis said the initial commitment is "50,000 dekatherms a day that can translate to up to 300 megawatts" and described a path to "a minimum of 200,000 dekatherms" enabling "up to 1.2 gigawatts."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment could not be directly assessed because the Q&A was presented as pre-submitted investor questions moderated by Zach Spencer, without third-party analysts speaking in the transcript.

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks around execution and positioning, but included visible caution and frustration on timing and visibility; CEO Corrado De Gasperis said, "we are a few months later than we thought" and on ramp guidance, "it's just still too nascent for us to be able to protect the ramp-up," while also expressing confidence in milestones like "we feel very confident running at the 25% level."

  • Versus Q1’s more assertive commissioning timeline (June/July operation), Q2’s tone showed more qualification and emphasis on sequencing and proof points before expansion; CEO De Gasperis stressed, "we won't make any decisions to deploy the next production equipment... until the first facility is up and running, until the first facility is running profitable."

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The operating timeline shifted: Q1 described customer visits tied to being able to "show them as soon as June" and said "By July, it will be operating continuously"; in Q2, CEO De Gasperis said commissioning would finish by end of July and operations would start in August, stating, "somewhere in August... it's going to be running at a certain level, and it's not going to stop."

  • Capital allocation discussion broadened in Q2 with more specific first-half deployment figures (Sierra Springs, facility completion, upgrades, R&D), while Q1 focused more on the financing completed and high-level estimates for facility #2 and recovery solutions.

  • The mining assets sale progressed from Q1’s "preliminary terms" and due diligence toward Q2’s expectation of closing and cash receipt; CFO Merrill said in Q2, "We do expect another $20 million in cash in August" tied to selling "100%" of legacy mining assets.

  • Q&A focus tightened further on near-term execution (facility start, capacity ramp, panel supply, and cash flow breakeven), and management repeatedly emphasized not being "premature" on deploying capital for additional facilities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Timing and ramp risk remained central: CEO De Gasperis acknowledged delay, stating, "we are a few months later than we thought," and cautioned on ramp visibility, saying, "it's just still too nascent for us to be able to protect the ramp-up."

  • Market/pricing uncertainty was flagged around metals: CEO De Gasperis said, "Silver price is down. So we're keeping a monitor on that," and separately emphasized that silver yields are still being validated, stating, "I don't think it's practical to talk about silver yields because that's what we're testing right now."

  • External process risk was noted on the mining asset sale, with CEO De Gasperis attributing timing to approvals: "The only thing that's holding that up... is TSX clearance."

Final Takeaway

Management positioned Q2 as a transition into operating execution, anchored by an August start for Comstock Metals’ first industry-scale solar panel recycling facility at an initial 25% capacity run rate, while tying cash flow positivity to utilization milestones. The company emphasized near-term liquidity and a debt-free balance sheet, and management expected an additional $20 million of cash in August from selling 100% of legacy mining assets, framing it as a simplification that removes reclamation liabilities while preserving royalty and equity upside. Alongside operations, management highlighted Sierra Springs as a monetization pathway driven by land consolidation and power procurement, and described Bioleum as being re-focused with a subsidiary capital raise timing described as a pause with a potential resumption in September/October.
